title: Switching-Cost Power

content.statement: Customers do not stay because they love you; they stay because leaving costs more than staying.
content.explainer: Switching costs are the friction of leaving: retraining, data migration, integrations, lost history. When that friction exceeds the annual value a customer gets, they stay even when a better option exists.
questions: If a competitor offered your customer the same value for free, what would it still cost them in time and disruption to actually switch?
Which of your switching costs are real lock-in you built (integrations, data, workflow) versus contract handcuffs a customer will resent?
Are you investing in raising switching costs, or quietly relying on inertia while a rival makes leaving effortless?
sop: List every switching cost a customer eats to leave you: retraining, data export, re-integration, lost history, and setup time.
Estimate the total dollar-and-hours cost of that switch for a typical customer.
Estimate the annual value that customer receives from you in dollars.
Divide switching cost by annual value to get your Lock-In score (see calculation, page 6).
Raise the number through embedded workflow and data, not through punitive contracts customers will escape at renewal.

definitions.def: Everything a customer spends — money, time, hassle, lost data or history — to move from you to another provider. Higher switching costs make leaving harder.
The degree to which a customer is held in place by the cost of leaving rather than by ongoing satisfaction.

content.equation_example: Switching Cost $6,000 ÷ Annual Value $2,000 = 3. A score above 1 means leaving costs more than a year of value, so the customer stays; below 1 means the exit is cheap and your retention is really just satisfaction.
content.closing_statement: Lock-in earned by being deeply useful is loyalty; lock-in enforced by penalty is a debt that comes due at renewal.
content.question: If a competitor offered your customer the same value for free, what would it still cost them in time and disruption to actually switch?

caption:
Principle Nº 349: Switching-Cost Power

Customers do not stay because they love you; they stay because leaving costs more than staying.

Ask yourself: If a competitor offered your customer the same value for free, what would it still cost them in time and disruption to actually switch?
